title: Chocolate babka
subtitle: Twisted Jewish enriched loaf with a dark chocolate filling
excerpt: An enriched yeasted loaf filled with dark chocolate and twisted before baking. The dough is rich with eggs and butter; the filling bakes into dark, molten seams that caramelise at the edges. Finished with a light sugar syrup. One 900 g tin.
sourceNotes: The babka is an Eastern European Jewish enriched bread tradition, documented in American public-domain household cookbooks of the early twentieth century (Internet Archive). Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg: enriched loaves with nut and dried-fruit fillings, twisted before baking, are documented in the chapter on celebratory breads. The chocolate filling is a twentieth-century development, replacing the earlier cinnamon-nut filling of the classic Polish babka, but the enriched dough and twisted shaping technique are historically documented. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845): the sweet enriched loaf chapter describes the shaping of twisted and rolled enriched doughs.

content.text: Babka is the enriched twisted loaf that makes the chocolate filling visible: the dough is rolled around it, cut in half lengthways, and the two halves twisted together before going into the tin. Where the layers separate during baking, the filling caramelises at the edges and the cut faces bake to a deep brown. The result looks intricate but the shaping is straightforward once you understand what you are doing.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and the {{caster-sugar}} for the dough in a large bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} and mix with the {{whole-milk}}. Pour into the flour and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 5 minutes, then begin adding the {{unsalted-butter}} for the dough in four additions, working each piece in fully before adding the next. The dough will seem greasy at first but will smooth out. Knead for a further 5 minutes until soft, glossy, and elastic. It will be quite sticky.
Cover the bowl and leave at room temperature for 90 minutes until roughly doubled. The butter makes this dough rise more slowly than a lean dough; 90 minutes is the minimum in a warm kitchen.

Melt the {{dark-chocolate}} and the {{unsalted-butter}} for the filling together over a low heat, stirring. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{caster-sugar}} for the filling and the {{cocoa-powder}}. Leave to cool to a spreadable paste. It should be thick enough to spread without running.

Butter the loaf tin. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough out to a rectangle about 35 × 25 cm. Spread the chocolate filling evenly to within 1 cm of the edges. Roll the dough tightly from a long edge into a log. Cut the log in half lengthways with a sharp knife to expose the filling. Lay the two halves side by side, cut-faces up. Twist them around each other twice, keeping the cut faces visible. This is the
. Lower the twisted loaf into the tin, tucking the ends under.
Cover loosely and leave for 60 minutes until puffed and risen to the top of the tin. Preheat the oven to 185°C fan.

Bake for 33 to 35 minutes until the top is deep brown and an internal probe reads 88°C. While the babka bakes, make the syrup: dissolve the {{caster-sugar}} for the syrup in the {{water}} in a small saucepan over medium heat, stirring once, then let it simmer for 2 minutes. As soon as the babka comes out of the oven, brush the syrup over the hot surface. The syrup soaks in as it cools, keeping the crumb moist. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out.
